lead: A business relocation lives or dies by its checklist. Furniture is the easy part; it is the leases, the IT, the staff and the hundred small admin jobs that catch firms out and turn a tidy move into lost trading days. Having helped Suffolk businesses relocate for many years, our crews have seen which tasks really matter and when they need doing. Here is a practical, ordered checklist for Suffolk businesses, grouped by stage, to keep your office move on track from first decision to first day trading.

sections.h: Three Months Out: Strategy and Premises
sections.body:
The earliest stage is about big decisions rather than boxes. Confirm the new premises, check the lease terms carefully, and pin down both your move-out date at the old site and your move-in date at the new one. Aim to overlap them by at least a few days; trying to be out of one building and into another on the same afternoon leaves no room for the inevitable hiccups.

Appoint a single move coordinator now and give them the authority to drive the project. They should build a master plan listing every major task, its owner and its deadline, so the whole relocation stays visible in one place. In our experience, the moment one accountable person can see the full picture, the dozens of small jobs stop slipping through the gaps.

Start budgeting too. A realistic figure covers not just the removals but any fit-out, new equipment, IT installation and the cost of any short overlap on rent. Walk the new space, check it suits how your teams actually work, and note anything that needs changing before staff arrive. Get the strategy right at this stage and everything that follows becomes simpler.

sections.h: Two Months Out: IT, Telephony and Suppliers
sections.body:
Technology has the longest lead times of anything in an office move, so tackle it early. Contact your internet provider about a new broadband or leased line at the new address, since these can take weeks to install and cannot be rushed on the day. Arrange number porting with your phone provider, and brief your IT support on what needs disconnecting, moving and reconnecting.

Work through your suppliers and service contracts. Some, such as cleaning, waste collection, water coolers and photocopier maintenance, will need transferring to the new address or cancelling at the old one. Note the notice periods, because contracts that quietly roll on at an empty building are money wasted. A simple list of every supplier and its status keeps this under control.

This is also the time to plan the new layout in detail. Agree where each team, meeting room, server and printer will go, and check that power and data points line up with the desk plan. Order any extra cabling, sockets or furniture now so it is in place before moving day. The clearer the plan, the faster our crews can set the office up exactly as you want it.

sections.h: One Month Out: Staff, Stationery and Address Changes
sections.body:
With a month to go, bring your people fully into the process. Share the timeline, explain the new layout, and ask each person to take responsibility for packing and labelling their own desk. Personal items go home; everything else goes into clearly marked crates by destination. Staff who know what is expected of them make a relocation run far more smoothly than those left in the dark.

Now begin the address changes in earnest. Update the website, email signatures, invoices, headed paper, business cards and every online listing. Notify the bank, HMRC, your insurers, key clients and suppliers, and set up a postal redirection to catch anything you miss. Done across a few weeks, this is straightforward; left to the final days, it becomes a scramble that follows you for months.

Order any new printed stationery showing the new address so it arrives in good time, and decide what existing stock can be used up first. Confirm the moving date and access arrangements with your removals team, flagging anything tricky about the loading or unloading at either site. By the end of this stage, the move should feel firmly under control.

The final week is for confirming, not starting. Reconfirm the date, time and access with your removals team, and check that the new broadband and phones will be live and tested before staff arrive. Make sure your IT team is booked to be on site at the new premises to bring systems up in the right order, and that everyone has back-ups in place as a precaution.

Walk both buildings one more time. At the old site, check the loading access, any parking restrictions and whether a lift needs booking. At the new one, confirm where the lorry can stop, that doorways and lifts will take your largest items, and that the floor plan is printed and ready. The fewer questions left open at this point, the calmer the day will be.

Brief your staff on the practicalities of the final days. Tell them clearly when to finish packing, where to leave their crates, what to take home and when they should report to the new address. If there will be any reduced service over the move, communicate it honestly to customers now rather than letting them discover it. Clear expectations all round prevent confusion when it matters most.

On the day, your coordinator runs the project while our crews handle the lifting and transport. Keep a trusted person at the old premises to oversee it until the last item is loaded, and another at the new site to direct the team and check everything against the inventory as it arrives. Have the floor plan to hand so furniture lands in the right place first time and never has to be moved twice.

Prioritise the things that get the business working again. The IT, the key workstations and the phones should be among the first items unloaded and set up, so essential staff can be operational while the rest of the office takes shape around them. Point out anything fragile or valuable, and let the crew work to the plan you have all agreed.

Before you hand back the old premises, do a thorough walk-through of every room, cupboard and store, and take final meter readings with a dated photo. At the new office, confirm that nothing is missing or damaged and that the layout matches the plan. With the heavy work done, the day ends with people more or less ready to start afresh.

The checklist does not quite end when the lorry leaves. In the first days at the new premises, work through the snagging list: anything that needs adjusting, kit that is not quite right, sockets or signage to sort. Tackle these promptly while everyone is fresh and the move is still front of mind, rather than letting small irritations linger into the routine of normal work.

Take meter readings at the new building, confirm all your supplier contracts have transferred correctly, and check that post is being redirected as it should. Make sure every address change has actually taken effect by looking at the live website, a recent invoice and a couple of online listings. It is easy to assume these are done; a quick verification saves embarrassment later.

Finally, gather feedback from your staff. They will spot the practical wrinkles in the new space faster than anyone, and addressing those quickly helps everyone feel settled. A relocation handled to the end of the checklist, not just to moving day, is one your team remembers as well run, and that goodwill is worth a great deal as the business finds its feet in its new Suffolk home.

faqs: What is the first step in an office relocation?

Confirm the new premises and lease, set firm move-out and move-in dates with a few days overlap, and appoint a single move coordinator with the authority to drive the project. Getting the strategy right early makes every later stage of the relocation simpler.
How early should I arrange IT and phones for an office move?

At least two months out. New broadband or leased lines can take weeks to install and cannot be rushed on the day, and phone number porting needs notice too. Aim for everything live and tested before staff arrive so the business can trade from day one.
What address changes do I need to make when relocating?

Update the website, email signatures, invoices, headed paper, business cards and online listings, and notify the bank, HMRC, insurers, clients and suppliers. Set up a postal redirection to catch anything you miss, and verify afterwards that each change has actually taken effect.
How do I minimise downtime during an office relocation?

Plan the new layout in advance, prioritise IT and phones so they are live first, and move over a weekend or quiet period. On the day, unload key workstations first so essential staff can work while the rest of the office is set up around them.
